comprehensive_summary:
Umberto Eco was an Italian semiotician, novelist, and essayist. His debut novel The Name of the Rose is set in a Benedictine abbey in 1327, where the Franciscan monk William of Baskerville (inspired by Sherlock Holmes, Thomas Aquinas, and Roger Bacon) and his novice Adso of Melk investigate a series of murders. William deduces that a missing horse is called Brunellus. The blind monk Jorge of Burgos has poisoned the pages of a lost second book of Aristotle's Poetics (on comedy), which he condemned because of its association with "infidel Moors" and because he believed comedy destroyed faith. The murders include Adelmo, Venantius (whose corpse is found in a vat of pig's blood), and Berengar. The secret room in the library is called the finis Africae. The inquisitor Bernard Gui arrests a peasant girl whom Adso had sex with (his first sexual experience) for witchcraft. Jorge kills himself by eating the poisoned pages and burning down the labyrinthine library. William tells Adso that the "epoch of penitence is over." The cellarer Salvatore frequently repeats "penitenziagite." The 1986 film adaptation stars Sean Connery and Christian Slater; Eco compared the novel to a "club sandwich." William Weaver translated the novel.

Foucault's Pendulum follows three editors -- Belbo, Diotallevi, and Casaubon -- who use a computer named Abulafia (after a Kabbalah philosopher) to construct connections between random words and build "The Plan," a fictional conspiracy theory about the Knights Templar. They use Colonel Ardenti's manuscript about the Knights Templar as the basis. The Plan involves telluric currents and the allegedly immortal Count Aglie (Saint-Germain). Other characters examine telluric currents. Eventually the creators come to believe The Plan is true, and Belbo is hanged from the titular scientific instrument in Paris. The Tres Society kills Belbo. The novel is divided into ten sections representing the ten Sefirot of the Kabbalah. Although named after Michel Foucault (the philosopher), the novel deliberately eschews any reference to him -- it refers to Leon Foucault's pendulum. A character divides the world into "cretins, fools, morons, and lunatics." Eco's depiction of Sergei Nilus adapts Danilo Kis's story "The Book of Kings and Fools."

Travels in Hyperreality is an essay collection. The title essay (originally called "Faith in Fakes") compares the LBJ Presidential Library, waxworks museums on Fisherman's Wharf, Superman's Fortress of Solitude, and Disneyland as examples of how "the American imagination demands the real thing and, to attain it, must fabricate the absolute fake." The opening section on "Fortresses of Solitude" discusses Wunderkammern (museums of memories). Eco discusses how holography "could only prosper in America" and analyzes Hearst Castle. He coined the term "fac-different" with an analogy to Asimov's laws of robotics. In "Travels in Hyperreality," he expanded Baudrillard's analysis of Disneyland.

How to Travel with a Salmon is another essay collection. The title essay describes how a hotel computer glitch causes him to be charged for minibar alcohol, which he had actually moved to store a smoked fish he bought in Stockholm. The collection also includes an essay about L'Avventura, one describing difficulties with a hotel clerk who spoke a dialect from "Kefiristan," and "How to Use the Coffeepot from Hell." Other essay collections include Misreadings (parodies including Columbus's landing on TV and a parody of Lolita about an old woman named Granita).

"Ur-Fascism" (or "Eternal Fascism") lists fourteen features of fascism, drawing on Eco's experience growing up in Mussolini's Italy. He uses Wittgenstein's family resemblance to describe the fourteen ways. The final principle claims "Ur-Fascism speaks Newspeak." He called for "communications guerilla warfare" in a separate essay.

The Prague Cemetery is a picaresque novel about "the most hateful man in the world," the antisemitic forger Simone Simonini (Captain Simonelli). Abbe Dalla Piccola alters Simonini's diary while he sleeps. The novel weaves together the Templars, the Illuminati, the Dreyfus Affair, and the Protocols of the Elders of Zion. Simonini is disguised as Abbe Dalla Piccola when he strangles Diana Vaughan at a Satanist orgy.

The Island of the Day Before follows the 17th-century explorer Roberto della Griva, who is marooned on a ship and believes he is on the International Date Line. He writes love letters to Lady Lilia and muses on his imaginary evil twin. A German Jesuit named Gaspar fails to teach him to swim after the shipwreck of the Daphne. He is tasked with discovering the secret of longitude.

Baudolino follows an Italian peasant who becomes the adopted son of Frederick Barbarossa. He recounts a fifteen-year search for Prester John to Niketas Choniates after saving him during the sack of Constantinople. A historical figure's corpse is thrown into a river after dying at an Armenian noble's estate.

The Mysterious Flame of Queen Loana follows Yambo, an amnesiac who loses his memories after a stroke. He discovers a Shakespeare First Folio among his grandfather's books, which causes him to relive lost memories.

Numero Zero is Eco's final novel, in which the paranoiac Braggadocio becomes convinced Mussolini survived WWII while hack journalists Colonna and another have an affair.

As an academic, Eco was a pioneer of semiotics. He proposed categories of recognition, ostension, replica, and invention in A Theory of Semiotics. He co-founded the journal Versus. The Open Work contrasted classical art with the aleatoric music of Stockhausen and Boulez. He argued that texts produce "model readers" in The Role of the Reader. He outlined nine possible plot moves in James Bond novels in "Narrative Structures in Fleming." He analyzed metaphor in Finnegans Wake. He conducted debates on "iconism" with Christian Metz, Roland Barthes, and Pier Paolo Pasolini. He wrote on Superman's myth. He analyzed Casablanca as using "intertextual archetypes." He delivered the Charles Eliot Norton lectures at Harvard. He called Ulysses a "summa turned upside down" in The Aesthetics of Chaosmos. He sometimes used the pseudonym Dedalus. He cameoed in the film La Notte. He proclaimed "the list is the origin of culture" in The Infinity of Lists.

works.name: The Name of the Rose
works.description: Eco's debut novel set in a Benedictine abbey in 1327. William of Baskerville and his novice Adso of Melk investigate murders caused by Jorge of Burgos, who poisoned the pages of Aristotle's lost second book of the Poetics (on comedy).

clues.clue: William of Baskerville and Adso of Melk investigate murders at a Benedictine abbey/monastery
examples: Adelmo's death prompts William of Baskerville and Adso of Melk to investigate a monastery.
This author wrote a novel in which Adso of Melk assists William of Baskerville in investigating a series of murders at a medieval abbey.

clues.clue: Jorge of Burgos poisons the pages of Aristotle's Poetics (lost second book on comedy); kills himself by eating the pages and burning down the library
examples: The monk Jorge of Burgos poisons the pages of Aristotle's Poetics in order to commit murders.
Jorge kills himself by burning down the library.
Jorge of Burgos commits suicide by eating the poisoned pages.

clues.clue: William deduces a missing horse is called Brunellus
examples: A character deduces that a missing horse is called Brunellus.
This author deduces the whereabouts of a horse named Brunellus.

clues.clue: The finis Africae is a secret room in the labyrinthine library
examples: The finis Africae is a secret room in the library of a Benedictine abbey.
William of Baskerville and Adso of Melk confront him in the finis Africae.

clues.clue: Adso's first sexual experience is with a peasant girl who is arrested for witchcraft by inquisitor Bernard Gui
examples: The protagonist has his first sexual experience with a peasant girl before that girl is arrested by inquisitor Bernard Gui.
A prostitute is accused of witchcraft by Bernard Gui.

clues.clue: Venantius's corpse found in a vat of pig's blood; Adelmo and Berengar also die
examples: Venantius's corpse is found in a vat of pig's blood before Bernardo Gui's inquisition.

clues.clue: William inspired by Sherlock Holmes, Thomas Aquinas, and Roger Bacon
examples: Medieval philosophers like Thomas Aquinas and Roger Bacon, along with Sherlock Holmes, inspired this author's character William of Baskerville.

clues.clue: 1986 film adaptation starring Sean Connery and Christian Slater; Eco compared it to a "club sandwich"
examples: This author compared one of his novels to a 'club sandwich' when discussing its 1986 adaptation starring Christian Slater and Sean Connery.

clues.clue: Salvatore repeats "penitenziagite"; William tells Adso the "epoch of penitence is over"
examples: Inspired by Fra Dolcino, the cellarer Salvatore frequently repeats a Latin command to perform this practice.

works.name: Foucault's Pendulum
works.description: Three editors -- Belbo, Diotallevi, and Casaubon -- use a computer named Abulafia to construct "The Plan," a fictional Knights Templar conspiracy theory that they eventually come to believe is true. Belbo is hanged from the pendulum in Paris.

clues.clue: Belbo, Diotallevi, and Casaubon develop "The Plan" using the computer Abulafia (named for a Kabbalah philosopher)
examples: Three men form 'The Plan' using a computer named for a Kabbalah philosopher, Abulafia.
Random sentences connected by the computer Abulafia are constructed into The Plan by Belbo and Casaubon.

clues.clue: Based on Colonel Ardenti's manuscript about Knights Templar; involves telluric currents
examples: Three editors use Colonel Ardenti's book about the Knights Templar as the basis for a fake conspiracy theory.
Several characters examine 'telluric currents' using the computer Abulafia.

clues.clue: Belbo is hanged from the titular pendulum in Paris; the Tres Society kills him
examples: Belbo is hanged from the titular scientific instrument in Paris.
A philologist witnesses the fictional Tres Society kill his friend.

clues.clue: Divided into ten sections representing the ten Sefirot of the Kabbalah
examples: Another of this author's works is divided into ten sections meant to represent the ten Sefirot of the Kaballah.

clues.clue: Named after Leon Foucault's pendulum, not the philosopher Michel Foucault; deliberately eschews reference to the philosopher
examples: Although that work is named after a famous French philosopher who was a close friend of this author, that work deliberately eschews any reference to him.

clues.clue: A character divides the world into "cretins, fools, morons, and lunatics"
examples: This author created a character who divides the world into 'cretins, fools, morons, and lunatics.'

clues.clue: The allegedly immortal Count Aglie (Saint-Germain) appears; a ghost exposes him as not actually being the Comte Saint-Germain
examples: A ghost exposes another character as not actually being the Comte Saint-Germain.
This novel's climax takes place at an industrial design museum.

works.name: Travels in Hyperreality / How to Travel with a Salmon
works.description: Two essay collections. "Travels in Hyperreality" compares the LBJ Library, waxworks, Disneyland, and Superman's Fortress of Solitude as examples of American "faith in fakes." "How to Travel with a Salmon" describes being charged for minibar alcohol after storing a fish.

clues.clue: "Travels in Hyperreality": compares LBJ Library, waxworks, Disneyland, Superman's Fortress of Solitude as "faith in fakes" / "the absolute fake"
examples: An essay by this author compares places like LBJ's Presidential Library and waxworks museums to Superman's Fortress of Solitude.
This author used Disneyland to illustrate artificial copies designed to be more real than their originals.

clues.clue: "How to Travel with a Salmon": charged for minibar alcohol after storing a smoked fish bought in Stockholm
examples: A hotel charges him for removing items of a minibar to stow smoked fish in it.
He recalls being charged for alcohol in a mini-bar, which he removed to make room for the title fish.

clues.clue: Holography "could only prosper in America"; expanded Baudrillard's analysis of Disneyland
examples: This author discussed how 'holography could only prosper in America.'
This author expanded Baudrillard's analysis of Disneyland in 'Travels in Hyperreality.'

clues.clue: Instructions on playing a Native American in a Western film and becoming a Knight of Malta
examples: A collection by this author includes instructions on playing a Native American in a Western film and becoming a Knight of Malta.

clues.clue: Albert Speer, Gaudi, LSD, and Liza Minnelli description of a place
examples: An essay by this author describes a place that looks like Albert Speer read a book on Gaudi, took LSD, and began to build a catacomb for Liza Minnelli.

works.name: Ur-Fascism
works.description: An essay listing fourteen features of fascism, drawing on Eco's experience growing up in Mussolini's Italy. Uses Wittgenstein's family resemblance concept.

clues.clue: Lists fourteen features/characteristics of fascism; draws on growing up in Mussolini's Italy
examples: This author used Wittgenstein's family resemblance to describe 'fourteen ways' in the essay 'Ur-Fascism.'
This author draws on his experience growing up in Mussoulini's Italy to develop a comprehensive definition of fascism built on 14 characteristics.

clues.clue: The final principle: "Ur-Fascism speaks Newspeak"
examples: The final principle of Eco's essay claims that 'Ur-Fascism speaks' this language.

clues.clue: Uses Wittgenstein's family resemblance concept
examples: This author used Wittgenstein's family resemblance to describe 'fourteen ways' in the essay 'Ur-Fascism.'

works.name: The Prague Cemetery
works.description: A picaresque novel about the antisemitic forger Simone Simonini ("the most hateful man in the world"). Abbe Dalla Piccola alters Simonini's diary while he sleeps. Weaves together the Templars, Illuminati, Dreyfus Affair, and the Protocols of the Elders of Zion.

clues.clue: About the antisemitic forger Simone Simonini, "the most hateful man in the world"; involves the Protocols of the Elders of Zion
examples: This picaresque novel about 'the most hateful man in the world,' the fictional antisemite Simone Simonini.
This author who wrote about the anti-semitic forger Captain Simonelli.

clues.clue: Abbe Dalla Piccola alters Simonini's diary entries while he sleeps
examples: Abbe adds to the diary of the forger, Simonini, while he sleeps.
Abbe Dalla Piccola alters the diary entries of the protagonist during his sleep.

clues.clue: Simonini disguised as Abbe Dalla Piccola strangles Diana Vaughan at a Satanist orgy
examples: A character disguised as Abbe Dalla Piccola strangles Diana Vaughan to death after impregnating her at a Satanist orgy.

works.name: The Island of the Day Before
works.description: A 17th-century explorer, Roberto della Griva, is marooned on a ship and believes he is on the International Date Line. He writes love letters to Lady Lilia, ponders his imaginary evil twin, and seeks the secret of longitude.

clues.clue: Roberto della Griva is marooned on a ship on the International Date Line; writes love letters to Lady Lilia; seeks the secret of longitude
examples: One of this author's protagonists is marooned on an island that he thinks is on the International Date Line.
Love letters to Lady Lilia are written by a man tasked with discovering the secret of longitude named Roberto della Griva.

clues.clue: A German Jesuit named Gaspar fails to teach the protagonist to swim after the shipwreck of the Daphne
examples: A German Jesuit named Gaspar fails to teach the protagonist to swim after the shipwreck of the Daphne.

works.name: Baudolino
works.description: An Italian peasant becomes the adopted son of Frederick Barbarossa and recounts a fifteen-year search for Prester John to the historian Niketas Choniates after saving him during the sack of Constantinople.

clues.clue: The protagonist recounts his search for Prester John to Niketas Choniates after saving him during the sack of Constantinople
examples: After rescuing a man from the sack of Constantinople, the protagonist recounts a fifteen-year search for Prester John.
This novel's title character tells his life story to Niketas Choniates after saving his life.

clues.clue: The protagonist is an Italian peasant who becomes the adopted son of Frederick Barbarossa
examples: Name this novel about an Italian peasant who becomes the adopted son of Frederick Barbarossa.

works.name: Other Novels
works.description: The Mysterious Flame of Queen Loana follows the amnesiac Yambo, who discovers a Shakespeare First Folio among his grandfather's books. Numero Zero features the paranoiac Braggadocio, who believes Mussolini survived WWII.

clues.clue: The Mysterious Flame of Queen Loana: amnesiac Yambo discovers Shakespeare's First Folio; relives lost memories of childhood
examples: A man discovers a picture book from which the novel takes its title before regaining part of his memory when he finds an original copy of the First Folio.
The amnesiac protagonist Yambo discovers Shakespeare's First Folio among his grandfather's books.

clues.clue: Numero Zero: Braggadocio believes Mussolini survived WWII; hack journalists have an affair; Eco's final novel
examples: Braggadocio becomes convinced that Mussolini survived World War II while two hack journalists have an affair.
The paranoiac Braggadocio informs the hack journalist Colonna that Mussolini survived World War II in Numero Zero.

works.name: General / Biographical and Academic Works
works.description: Italian semiotician who pioneered film semiotics and interpretative semiotics. He wrote The Open Work, A Theory of Semiotics, The Role of the Reader, and co-founded the journal Versus. He also wrote on beauty and ugliness, on lists, and on Superman's myth.

clues.clue: The Open Work: contrasted classical art with aleatoric music of Stockhausen and Boulez; "openness" as central goal of art
examples: This writer contrasted the unambiguous nature of 'classical' art with the aleatoric music of Stockhausen and Boulez, which he called 'open work.'
His best known aesthetic work extolled 'openness' as central goal of art.

clues.clue: Texts produce "model readers" (The Role of the Reader)
examples: According to this author, texts are devices to produce 'model readers.'
He suggested that texts are networks of messages that are designed to be actualized by a Model Reader.

clues.clue: A Theory of Semiotics: proposed categories of recognition, ostension, replica, and invention
examples: This Italian thinker proposed the categories of recognition, ostension, replica, and invention in A Theory of Semiotics.

clues.clue: Co-founded the journal Versus for the study of signs
examples: He founded the journal Versus.
An Italian author who co-founded the journal Versus for the study of these things.

clues.clue: Outlined nine possible plot moves in James Bond novels in "Narrative Structures in Fleming"
examples: In one essay, this author outlined the nine possible plot moves that can occur in the storyline of a James Bond novel.

clues.clue: Casablanca essay: cited Count of Monte Cristo as literary predecessor of "intertextual archetypes" that made it a cult film
examples: An essay cites The Count of Monte Cristo, 'one of the most badly written novels,' as a literary predecessor of the 'intertextual archetypes' that made Casablanca a cult film.
He decried Casablanca as a random assortment of unrelated concepts.

clues.clue: Proclaimed "the list is the origin of culture" in The Infinity of Lists
examples: This author proclaimed that 'the list is the origin of culture' and wrote the illustrated essay collection 'The Infinity of Lists.'

clues.clue: Cameoed in the film La Notte; conducted debates on "iconism" with Metz, Barthes, and Pasolini
examples: This theorist cameoed as himself in the book signing scene in La Notte.

clues.clue: Called Ulysses a "summa turned upside down" in The Aesthetics of Chaosmos
examples: He called Ulysses a 'summa turned upside down' in his The Aesthetics of Chaosmos.

clues.clue: Work on Thomas Aquinas led to The Development of Medieval Aesthetics; lost faith after this work
examples: This thinker claimed to be rid of his faith after his work on Thomas Aquinas which led him to write The Development of Medieval Aesthetics.

clues.clue: William Weaver translated many of his works
examples: Weaver translated Baudolino, Foucault's Pendulum, The Name of the Rose.
William Weaver translated this author's essay collection How To Travel With A Salmon.

clues.clue: Called for "communications guerilla warfare" in an essay collected in Travels in Hyperreality
examples: This writer called for a 'communications guerilla warfare' in an essay collected in Travels in Hyperreality.

summary: Umberto Eco was an Italian semiotician, novelist, and essayist. His debut novel The Name of the Rose is set in a Benedictine abbey in 1327, where the Franciscan monk William of Baskerville (inspired by Sherlock Holmes, Thomas Aquinas, and Roger Bacon) and his novice Adso of Melk investigate a series of murders.
